implausible_knowledge: “She christened the window perch, inspected all my books, and decided my favorite chair is now her chair. She ate, used the litter box like she’s been doing it forever, and took a nap on my lecture notes.”

story:
Adoption counselor Jan Mulford skimmed one more time through the application on her screen. Mark Hazlett, 35, English professor. Lifetime dog person, currently kept an aquarium of fish. He wanted Miss Kitty—the shelter’s affectionate calico Jan had secretly imagined taking home herself. She had already walked Mark through the practicals: food and litter costs, routine vet care, emergencies. Satisfied he’d done his homework, she went to meet him.

She found him at the viewing windows, tall and easy-shouldered, watching the cats with a thoughtful attention she liked. He turned at the sound of her steps and smiled—broad and unguarded, the kind that reached his clear green eyes.

“Mark?” she asked.

“Yes. Jan?” He offered a hand. “Thanks for seeing me.”

“Of course.” She gestured down the hall. “If you’re ready, we can meet Miss Kitty in one of the socialization rooms.”

He nodded eagerly. “Very.”

Jan keyed into the cat colony. A tide of meows lifted as the residents hurried to greet her, winding around her calves, batting at her lanyard. “I know, I know,” she laughed, crouching to rub a few heads. Miss Kitty—plush patches of cinnamon and cream—rose from a sunspot and came straight to her. Jan scooped the calico into a carrier, and just as the latch clicked, Gina slipped through the door with a grin.

“So this is Mr. Nice Smile?” Gina murmured, waggling her brows.

Heat climbed Jan’s neck. “Gina. Professionalism.”

“Professionalism can admire good dentistry,” Gina whispered. “For the record, marital status isn’t on the forms, but eyes can be.”

Jan rolled her eyes, shooed Gina with a laugh, and carried Miss Kitty back.

In the small room, she set the carrier on the floor and opened it. Miss Kitty flowed out, tail up like a flag, and made a beeline for Mark. He dropped to a knee, palm open, and Miss Kitty pressed her cheek into his hand with a pleased chirp. Jan, leaning against the wall, noticed he wasn’t wearing a ring.

“So,” she said, mostly to steady herself, “what shifted you from dogs to…this?” She nodded toward the calico now trying to sit in Mark’s lap.

He looked a little sheepish. “Last week I came here for a dog. I was hovering by the kennels, undecided, when I glanced over and saw you in the cat room with her.” He stroked behind Miss Kitty’s ear. “Miss Kitty was melting under your hand like it was the whole point of the day. I hadn’t realized a cat could want you that way.”

Jan’s laugh came out softer than she intended. “Most do. They just make you earn it first.”

“I went home and read everything I could find about litter boxes and scratching posts and feeding schedules,” he went on. “It turns out the syllabus for Cat 101 is a lot more interesting than grading papers. And I kept thinking about her. I figured if she was still here, it was meant to be.”

“Well,” Jan said, watching the animal and the man in a snug little loop of contentment, “I did consider taking Miss Kitty myself.” The words surprised her as much as they seemed to surprise him. She lifted a shoulder, honest and a touch rueful. “But I’m glad you came back. I think she’s choosing you.”

“I’m sorry if I’m stealing someone you love,” Mark said. “I’m ready to do it right, though. I already bought food, a scratching post, a litter setup—the works.” He offered an embarrassed smile. “And there’s a carrier in my trunk that I probably don’t need now that you’ve got a nicer one.”

“Then let’s make this official,” Jan said.

They finished the paperwork quickly. While Mark ran out to bring in his carrier, Jan crouched to rub Miss Kitty’s chin. “You little heart thief,” she murmured. “You’re going to break all our routines.”

Gina popped her head through the door again. “Congratulations, Mr. Nice Smile,” she sang toward the hallway. Then, to Jan, sotto voce: “If our new cat dad has questions, you’d be happy to help. Maybe even by phone? Don’t waste those curls.”

“Out,” Jan hissed, laughing despite herself.

Mark returned, and Gina vanished like smoke. He set his carrier down and looked at Jan, suddenly hesitating. “Do you have a cat at home?”

“I do,” she said. “A tabby tyrant.”

“Perfect. I’m a first-time cat owner.” He glanced at the paperwork, then back at her. “If I have questions, could I—would you be willing to meet up? Maybe…over dinner?”

It wasn’t the first time someone had flirted with an adoption counselor, but it was the first time Jan felt the tug in her own chest. She swallowed, surprised by the flutter and by how right the yes felt as it rose.

“I’d like that,” she said.

That evening they met at Wildcat Cafe, the university’s unofficial second faculty lounge, where the walls held decades of posters and the booths had learned the contours of a thousand stake-your-claim conversations. Jan’s hair had lost a little of its afternoon frizz; Mark had swapped his rumpled button-down for one without chalk on it.

“She christened the window perch, inspected all your books, decided your favorite chair is now hers, like she’s been doing it forever,” Jan said once the menus were closed and the tea arrived.

“Exploring,” he said, grinning, then blinking at her in pleased amazement. “Exactly.” He paused, the humor softening to something else. “Thank you, Jan. For bringing us together.”

“She did most of the work,” Jan said, unable to keep the warmth out of her voice.

He fiddled with his cup, then met her eyes. “If I’m being honest, I might never have wandered into the cat room that first day if I hadn’t noticed the woman with the brown, curly hair making a little calico look like the center of the universe.”

Jan felt the certainty settle in her, gentle but firm, like a cat stepping into a lap that has always been waiting. Maybe her goodbye to Miss Kitty hadn’t been goodbye at all—just a different kind of beginning.
